---
title: "corpus-caesarianum-authorship"
output:
  html_document:
    df_print: paged
editor_options:
  markdown:
    wrap: 72
---

This is an [R Markdown](http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com) Notebook. When you
execute code within the notebook, the results appear beneath the code.

```{r include=TRUE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(results = "hold")
knitr::opts_knit$set(root.dir = 'C:/Users/T470s/Documents/GitHub/corpus-caesarianum-authorship')
```

# Data Preparation

```{r Libraries}
# install.packages("factoextra")
# install.packages("dplyr")
# install.packages("ggrepel")
# install.packages("tidyverse")
# install.packages("dendextend")
# install.packages("MASS")
# install.packages("ggtext")

library(factoextra)
library(dplyr)
library(ggrepel)
library(tidyverse)
library(tidytable)
library(cluster)
library(combinat)
library(dendextend)
library(MASS)
library(multcomp)
library(ggtext)
```

(For the above ggrepel library, see
<https://github.com/slowkow/ggrepel>)

```{r Get postagged_only.csv. Big data!}

dims <- c(
  "title", 
  "author", 
  "cite1",
  "cite2",
  "path",
  "form",
  "lemma",
  "tag",
  "Aspect",
  "Mood",
  "Number",
  "Person",
  "Tense",
  "VerbForm",
  "Voice",
  "Case",
  "PronType",
  "Gender",
  "Polarity",
  "Degree",
  "NumType",
  "Deprel",
  "parent_form",
  "parent_lemma",
  "parent_tag",
  "parent_Aspect",
  "parent_Mood",
  "parent_Number",
  "parent_Person",
  "parent_Tense",
  "parent_VerbForm",
  "parent_Voice",
  "parent_Case",
  "parent_PronType",
  "parent_Gender",
  "parent_Polarity",
  "parent_Degree",
  "parent_NumType",
  "parent_Deprel"
)

data <- read.csv("postagged-texts.csv", na.strings = "", col.names = dims, stringsAsFactors = TRUE)

#This isn't a ratio or count variable
data <- data |>
  mutate(Person = as.factor(Person))

summary(data)
```

Add book numbers, if applicable. I add them to a separate column and the
title; a bit unclean, but I'll have to fix it later.

```{r}

get_book <- function(title, cite1, cite2) {
  if (title == "De bello Gallico") {
    return(substr(x=cite2, start=1, stop=1))
  }
  else if (title == "De Bello Civili") {
    return(cite1)
  }
  else {
    return(0)
  }
}

data <- data |>
  rowwise() |>
  mutate(book = get_book(title, cite1, cite2)) |>
  relocate(book, .before = form)

# data <- data |>
#   rowwise() |>
#   mutate(title = paste(title, "_", book))
# 
# data <- tidytable::select(data, -book)
```

Now let's get the section number.

```{r}
get_sec <- function(title, cite1, cite2) {
  if (title == "De bello Gallico") {
    return(unlist(
      strsplit(as.character(cite2), '[.]')
    )[2])
  }
  else if (title == "De Bello Civili") {
    return(cite2)
  }
  else {
    return(cite1)
  }
}

data <- data |>
  rowwise() |>
  mutate(section = get_sec(title, cite1, cite2)) |>
  relocate(section, .before = form)
```

```{r}

# Rename "Imp" to "Imperative" in mood category
levels(data$Mood) <- c(levels(data$Mood), "Imperative")
sequence <- which(data$Mood == "Imp")
data$Mood[sequence] <- "Imperative"
remove(sequence)

data$Mood[data$Mood == "Imp"] <- "Imperative"
data$Mood <- droplevels(data$Mood) # Remove the old "Imp

data <- data |>
  mutate(expanded_title = )

titles <- unique(data$title)
num_words <- table(data$title)
final.frame <- data.frame(title = titles)
```

Now that we have the data, what we're *really* interested in is how many
of each tag appears in each work (how many of each POS, how many perfect
verbs, whatever). The point is, we're not interested in every individual
word.

NOTE: I've created a heading (currently empty) down below where we test
formatting variables like Gorman 2016.

This block of code will loop through and create a new data frame called
***final.frame*** (which is probably poorly named, but I'll fix it later
or I won't). This data frame has a variable for the count of each tag,
and every row is a unique work.

```{r Create final.frame}



for (i in 1:length(titles)) {
    frame <- data[data$title == titles[i], ]
    frame <- subset(frame, select = c(-form, -path, -NumType, -author, -lemma, -cite1, -cite2))
    for (n in 2:length(frame)) {
      t <- table(frame[, ..n])
      # t <- prop.table(t)
      names <- names(t)
  
      for (name_index in seq(1, length(names))) {
        final.frame[i, names[name_index]] <- unname(t[names[name_index]])
      }
    }
}

# Normalize by number of words in each work
for (i in 2:length(final.frame[1, ])) {
  for (n in 1:length(final.frame[, 1])) {
    final.frame[n, i] <- final.frame[n, i] * (10000 / num_words[[final.frame[n, ]$title]])
  }
}
```

A few things will make our life easier. We don't want any NAs in there,
because that'll gum up the clustering. We'll replace any missing values
with 0, because 0 occurrences of a variable is the only reason there
would be NA values at this stage.

The numbers aren't normalized according to the size of their respective
works yet, so I've divided each number by the total number of words in
the given work in the next chunk. I multiplied each by 100,000 too, just
to get numbers that look a little nicer and aren't in scientific
notation.

Finally, we do what I said above, turning the rownames into the title. I
shorten them to 12 characters max.

```{r Clean up final.frame}
# Removed punctuation because PCA showed that it was an "important" variable
final.frame <- subset(final.frame, select = c(-PUNCT, -X))

# Clean up NAs
final.frame[is.na(final.frame)] <- 0

#Add rownames
# 
# for (i in 1:length(final.frame$title)) {
#   final.frame$title <- as.character(final.frame$title)
#   final.frame$title[i] <- paste(as.character(i), final.frame$title[i])
# }
# 
# rownames(final.frame) <- final.frame$title
# final.frame$title <- lapply(X = final.frame$title, FUN = substr, start = 1, stop = 16)
# rownames(final.frame) <- lapply(X = rownames(final.frame), FUN = substr, start = 1, stop = 16)
```

Now that we have a viable DF, let's cut it up a little bit. We don't
want the first column, because that's all text. Not every clustering
algorithm needs the DF to be pre-scaled (that is, subtracting the mean
from every value and dividing it by the standard deviation to make the
comparable). However, some do expect the data to be scaled, so lets
create two variables. **df_clust** isn't scaled, and **df_scaled** is.

```{r df_clust & df_scaled}
df_clust <- final.frame[, 2:length(final.frame)]
rownames(df_clust) <- final.frame$title
# df_clust <- slice_sample(df_clust, n=45)

df_scaled <- as.data.frame(scale(df_clust))
```

# Analysis

First, to get some of the formatting predefined.

Another note is that here is where I remove the variables I'm not
considering.

```{r}
# These vars I selected based on the Better variable selection heading down below.
# These are the variables that belonged to the 30 with the highest frequency AND the highest variance
# I also removed Act because it isn't independent from passive verbs, and I removed gender because there's no reason to believe it's related to register.
# vars <- c("Pres", "NOUN", "Imp", "2", "Past", "Perf", "3", "PRON", "Fin", "Nom", "Abl", "Prs", "Acc", "ADJ", "Gen", "ADP", "Part", "Pass", "DET", "Rel")
# 
# df_clust <- df_clust %>% dplyr::select(all_of(vars))
# rownames(df_clust) <- rownames(final.frame)
# df_scaled <- df_scaled %>% dplyr::select(all_of(vars))
# rownames(df_scaled) <- rownames(df_scaled)
```

```{r}


# Add this with the + operator to fviz_cluster plots. @param data is the original dataset used for cluster analysis (df_clust in the first case)
cluster_labels <- geom_text_repel(aes(label = rownames(data)), vjust = "inward", hjust = "inward", max.overlaps = 20, size = 3)
# Args for fviz_clust
palette <- c("#2E9FDF", "#00AFBB", "#E7B800", "red", "gray")
geom <- "point"
ellipse.type <- "convex"
ggtheme <- theme_bw()

# K-means
k <- 3

# Hcut
hc_func <- "agnes"
hc_method <- "ward"
hc_metric <- "euclidean"
cex <- 0.2

# Text labels for distance diagrams. @param label is usually the rownames of a data.frame
margin_size <- 10
distance_labels <- theme(axis.text = element_text(size = 5, vjust = 1), margins = margin(t = margin_size, r = margin_size, b = margin_size, l = margin_size))

# Dendrogram theme
dend_size <- 0.2
dend_theme <- theme(axis.text = element_text(size = dend_size, vjust = 1), margins = margin(t = margin_size, r = margin_size, b = margin_size, l = margin_size))
```

## K-means clustering

Time to cluster! K-means is a good starting point here. I'll check out
CLARA as well. We'll need to evaluate what's best. Here's the
modifications we can make:

1.  Change which variables we account for.

2.  Change the number of centers (between 3 and 5? But we already have a
    way of visualizing that)

3.  Change the measuring method (i.e. Euclidean

4.  Change the algorithm

```{r K-means clustering}
set.seed(126126)
kmeans_basic_cluster <- kmeans(df_clust, centers = k, nstart = 25)
kmeans_basic_table <- data.frame(kmeans_basic_cluster$size, kmeans_basic_cluster$centers)
kmeans_basic_df <- data.frame(Clusters = kmeans_basic_cluster$cluster, df_clust)
```

Let's do our first visualization. Four clusters seems like a reasonable
amount, looking at the fviz_nbclust results with the wss method.

```{r}
# TODO ADD SUMMARIES OF THE ABOVE DATA HERE

# Principal component analysis

pca <- prcomp(df_clust)

summary(pca)

# Visualizations

fviz_nbclust(df_clust, kmeans, method = "wss", nstart = 50)

dim1 <- as.character(summary(pca)$importance[2] * 100)
dim2 <- as.character(summary(pca)$importance[5] * 100)

cluster_main <- fviz_cluster(kmeans_basic_cluster,
  data = df_clust,
  palette = palette,
  geom = geom,
  ellipse.type = ellipse.type,
  ggtheme = ggtheme
) +
  geom_text_repel(
    aes(label = rownames(df_clust)),
    vjust = "inward",
    hjust = "inward",
    max.overlaps = 14,
    size = 2,
    seed = 12927
  ) +
  labs(
    x = paste(strwrap(paste("Dim1", dim1, "(Left = more 1st/2nd person, imperfective verbs, Right = more Ablative nouns, perfective verbs, and nouns overall)"), 70),
      collapse = "\n"
    ),
    y = paste(strwrap(paste("Dim2", dim2, "(Up = more 3rd person and past tense verbs, Down = more nouns)"), 50), collapse = "\n"),
    title = "(1) K-means clustering applied to PHI Latin Texts, k=4"
  ) +
  theme(axis.title = element_text(size = 12))

cluster_main


ggsave(filename = "Images/cluster-diagram-kmeans.png", dpi = "print")

str(kmeans_basic_cluster)

```

```{r}


distance <- get_dist(df_scaled)
fviz_dist(distance, gradient = list(low = "#00AFBB", mid = "white", high = "#FC4E07")) + distance_labels + theme(axis.text = element_text(size = 2))

fviz_dist(distance, gradient = list(low = "#00AFBB", mid = "white", high = "#FC4E07")) + distance_labels + theme(axis.text = element_text(size = 2))

ggsave(filename = "Images/Distance plot with main d.f.png", dpi = "print")
```

The graph above should be the first visualization in the presentation.
Next, we want to see what sets the Letters and other similar texts apart
from the others.

The following graph represents the loadings for the X axis of the
cluster analysis above. The farther we go to one side, the more 1st/2nd
person verbs, personal pronouns, present/future tense verbs, and
imperfective aspect verbs there are. Let's talk about what that means.

Person corresponds to a change in tenor accompanied by colloquial texts.
Tenor, as a term, refers to the author and their relationship to their
audience. Comedy, letters, and works of Seneca which come very close to
that cluster have an intimate audience (in the case of Comedy and
Seneca, this is an affectation. In such a close situation, the goal is
to both get information from the addressee but also communicate
information about themselves.

This information about person is fairly obvious, but the situation with
personal pronouns is less obvious. Latin has no need of personal
pronouns; the grammars say they tend to be used for emphasis or
...(*cite Allen & Greenough and Kühner / Stegmann*).

The information about tense is also expected. Speakers in colloquial
texts have a variety of purposes: asking and answering questions,
demonstrating their current thoughts and feelings, making plans, and so
on. These cover a broad range of statements, each of which requires a
different type of verb (*examples here*).

To sum up, texts on the left-hand side of the graph have a highly
personal tenor with ....FILL IN HERE, RE-READ BIBER

Texts on the the right-hand side of the cluster graph feature more
ablative nouns, participles, and passive verbs.

```{r}
pca <- prcomp(df_clust)

rotations.df <- as.data.frame(pca$rotation)

rotations <- data.frame(var = rownames(rotations.df), rotations = rotations.df$PC1)

rotation.two <- data.frame(var = rownames(rotations.df), rotations = rotations.df$PC2)

ggplot(rotations, aes(var, rotations)) +
  labs(x = "Variables", y = "Correlation Strength", title = "(2) Variation across X-axis in cluster diagram") +
  geom_col() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 15, angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust = 1),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14),
    title = element_text(size = 14)
  )
ggsave(filename = "Images/PC1_rotations.png", dpi = "print")

ggplot(rotations, aes(var, rotations)) +
  labs(x = "Variables", y = "Correlation Strength", title = "(2) Variation across X-axis in cluster diagram") +
  geom_col() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 15, angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust = 1),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14),
    title = element_text(size = 14)
  )
ggsave(filename = "Images/PC1_rotations.png", dpi = "print")

ggplot(rotation.two, aes(var, rotations)) +
  labs(x = "Variables", y = "Correlation Strength", title = "Correlation of Y-axis in cluster diagram with selected variables") +
  geom_col() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 15, angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust = 1)
  ) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 15, angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust = 1)
  )
ggsave(filename = "Images/PC2_rotations.png", dpi = "print")

# NOTE: I should try this with Act as a variable too. Pass alone might be high because there are a lot of participles and ablative nouns.
```

How different are the values of Atticus compared to the means of its own
cluster?

```{r}
# Lets get a vector of each of the Att values minus the mean of its cluster.

cluster_four <- kmeans_basic_df |> filter(Clusters == 1)

dist <- cluster_four |>
  dplyr::select(-title) |>
  map(
    \(x) x[1] - mean(x[-1])
  )

dist_df <- data.frame(var = names(dist)[-1], distance = unlist(dist)[-1])

ggplot(dist_df, aes(var, distance)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_continuous(breaks = c(0, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250)) +
  labs(title = "(3) Difference Between Letters to Atticus and Its Cluster") +
  xlab("Variable (X1 and X2 = 1st person and 2nd person)") +
  ylab("Letters to Atticus minus cluster mean") +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 15, angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust = 1),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14),
    title = element_text(size = 14)
  )

ggsave(filename = "Images/att-vs-same-cluster-means.png", dpi = "print")
```

What about the differences between Comedy and the Letters to Atticus?

```{r}
kmeans_basic_df$title <- as.vector(final.frame$title)
cluster_four <- kmeans_basic_df |>
  filter(Clusters == 4 | title == "1 Letters to Att") |>
  dplyr::select(-title)

dist <- cluster_four |>
  map(
    \(x) x[1] - mean(x[-1])
  )

dist_df <- data.frame(var = names(dist)[-1], distance = unlist(dist)[-1])

ggplot(dist_df, aes(var, distance)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_continuous(breaks = c(0, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250)) +
  labs(title = "(4) Difference Between Letters to Atticus and Comedy") +
  xlab("Variable (X1 and X2 = 1st person and 2nd person)") +
  ylab("Letters to Atticus minus cluster mean") +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = 15, angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust = 1),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14),
    title = element_text(size = 14)
  )

ggsave(filename = "Images/atticus-vs-comedy-means.png", dpi = "print")
```

## Exploratory Visualization

We need to understand the variables before we go into much more detail.
Which ones are the most frequent? Which ones vary the most? Do we have a
lot of covariates? I'll establish a list of procedures in more detail
later, but, for now, just seeing the frequency of each variable by text
would be desirable

```{r}

```

## Gorman-Style Variables

```{r}
"pass"
```
